# 11 Brutal Truths To Grow Your Business Fast — Raw Transcript

*Source: YouTube video by Sabri Suby (founder of King Kong, a $250M+ agency; Shark Tank Australia). Auto-generated transcript, lightly de-noised; profanity redacted as `[__]` in the original dump. Distilled into entries listed in the Backlinks pane.*

---

No one cares about you, your company, or how hard you've worked. People only care about themselves and their outcome. And once you accept it, you can start printing money. But most people never accept it. And then they build something that no one ever asks for. And they waste years of their life working on the wrong things. And by the end of this video, you'll have all the brutal truths that decide who actually makes money and who stays broke.

**Brutal truth #1 — No one gives a [__] about you.** This took me way longer than I care to admit to actually figure out. They don't care how long you've been in business, how hard you're working, how good you think your products are. The only thing people care about is themselves and what you're selling — how it's going to help them in their exact situation. The market is a weighing scale. It has no emotions. The only way it works is transacting based on value. I learned this in my first sales role on the front lines of capitalism, getting my teeth kicked in, in an old converted shipping container crammed with 16 people, manually dialing an old-school telephone trying to sell ink cartridges. I'd give this big spiel about the company I worked for and how we were saving the planet and the turtles — and I'd get hung up on all day. It wasn't until I changed my pitch to telling people I would buy back their empty ink cartridges and wire them $500, and they wouldn't have to do anything other than read me the serial number on the side of their printer, that everything changed. They only care about how it impacts their life. This one shift took me from the runt of the litter — the worst salesperson they were about to get rid of — to the number one salesperson at every company I've ever worked at. The market doesn't reward people by how much they care. It rewards them for solving problems.

**Brutal truth #2 — The game is 80% mindset.** When building a business you're going to be hiring people, dealing with lots of clients — and people are going to rip you off, cheat you, lie to you. That's just the cost of doing business. Business is 20% mechanics and 80% mindset. A lot of people who built big businesses did it because they just never quit. The big thing is: what is your tolerance to risk? The most extreme example on Earth is Elon Musk — he sold PayPal, put all of it into SpaceX and the rest into Tesla, and started sleeping on his parents' couch again. That's all-in. The longer you can delay gratification, the bigger the hit you'll make. I lost a $35,000 website deal because the person wanted to come into my office, which I didn't have. I walked into the forest, had a meeting with myself: keep doing this little cash-flow side hustle from my bedroom, or build a bigger business? I decided to build a bigger business. So I got an office, fit it out, paid a deposit, started building a team. There's a point I call death valley — do I keep doing this myself and work like a maniac, or hire people when I don't have much money? The answer is you have to do both: work like a maniac to afford to hire people and bring them in. The higher you climb, the harder it gets, the bigger the target on your back — you just get better.

**Brutal truth #3 — Unless you hire a team, you don't have a business, you have a job.** Business owners always complain they can't find good people, so they never create a business — they create a job, working 80–120 hours a week for basically minimum wage, because their comparison stick is always their own level of efficiency. It is okay to hire somebody who can do the role 50% as good as you. Would you rather have one person who can do the job at 100%, or 10 people who can do it at 50%? When I started I was doing all the selling by day and all the client work (the "farming") by night — cold calling all day, then client work in the evening — to the point where I had 70 clients. Madness. When I hired my first account manager, I realized very quickly they didn't need to go as deep as me. That account manager bought me back 100 hours a month, which I spent growing the business — hiring salespeople, building a sales team, creating a unified sales process. If I'd never let go, I'd still be a small agency scratching it out with the turkeys, holding myself back thinking no one can do this job as well as me.

**Brutal truth #4 — The money is not in your thing, it's in the selling of your thing.** In business, sales trumps all. The money isn't in your goods and services — it's in the selling of them. If you can sell better than anyone in the marketplace, you make the most money; then you can hire the best talent; they service clients best and create the best goods to sell. Having distribution is a terminal monopoly on a market, because often it's the best-known product that wins, not the best product. Imagine two founders: one is product-obsessed, toils in his room making the best product; the other is execution- and distribution-obsessed. The execution-focused one beheads the product guy every day of the week. Who has arguably the best products in the world? Apple. Steve Jobs was one of the greatest salespeople to ever live — "a thousand songs in your pocket." He famously spent ~80% of his time on recruiting, ~10–15% on sales and marketing, ~5% on actual product. The cash he generated let him hire Jony Ive to build the best products. You don't build the product then think "how do I sell this?" You should already know how you'll sell it before you develop it.

**Brutal truth #5 — If your offer doesn't keep you up at night, it isn't strong enough.** Most people don't have an offer. Even if they do, it's far from irresistible. Running a landing page or some traffic that says "get a quote and buy my stuff" is not an offer. "We're going to guarantee to get you to this outcome, or you don't take the risk — we'll burden the risk." That's an offer. When I tell owners this, the first objection is "that's too risky" — but not having an offer is already costing your business: you're running ads, paying salespeople, but your conversion rate is dog [__]. When you have a compelling offer that keeps you up at night — "holy [__], how am I going to deliver on this?" — way more people buy, because they're thinking "how is this company able to do this?" That's when your CAC becomes one-third of what it is now. So think about what they want more than anything, get hyper-specific, attach a number and an outcome. Then add a hero mechanism — this is how it's different from every other offer in the marketplace — so you're in a category of one and can't be itemized and compared against competitors.

**Brutal truth #6 — F[__] your ROAS.** I ask businesses what's working. "I'm spending $5k on ads." Have you tried spending more? "I went from $5k to $10–15k a month, and my ROAS dropped from a 5 to a 4." F[__] your ROAS — you can't pay overhead with ROAS. Would you rather spend $5k/month at a 5x ROAS, or $50k/month at a 3.5x ROAS? The only thing that matters is how much net cash you have at the end of the month. When you spend $5k on ads, that's 5,000 little money soldiers — all you care about is that they return to camp with more money soldiers. It's not the percentage they grew; it's how many come back. If ROAS alone were the goal, every guru posting 1,000% ROAS would be a billionaire. Same in services: "my cost per booked call is $180; if I spend more it goes to $210." What if you get 150 more leads? "We'd do more business, but I don't want to go over $200 per call." Why? That's an arbitrary limitation you plant on yourself that becomes the constraint. Think about how big you want to build the business, then think about the plays to allocate that capital — hiring, new geography, new category. Coming from the guy with a Ferrari: I could afford five Ferraris for many years before I bought one, because I took that money and invested it into the business, the team, offices, new markets — so one Ferrari's worth of money became five. Delay the gratification. Even if vanity metrics (CPL, cost per booked call, ROAS) dip, that's okay — you're making more net free cash flow.

**Brutal truth #7 — Most businesses die from indigestion, not starvation.** People try multiple businesses, have multiple failures, finally find one that works — a selling system where they put $1 into ads and $5 comes back. The moment it works at limited scale, they think "I've got this golden goose, let me leave it here and go find another goose in the forest because I heard this other guy is running a quiz funnel." Don't say "hey little goose, I'm going to go find another one of you." Treat that thing like a god, because it's creating net free cash flow. The worst thing that can happen is you catch a bad case of SOS — shiny object syndrome — always looking on the perimeter for something shinier, sexier, easier, diluting your focus. What serves you is laser-like focus on optimizing the current system that's already working. Make hay while the sun shines — don't take for granted that market conditions stay positive forever. When you find something that works, crank that sucker down and ride the wave.

**Brutal truth #8 — The fishing is best where the fewest go.** Everybody is funnel-hacking other people's funnels, living in the Facebook ads library, copying and pasting every move. The fishing is best where the fewest go. When I started, I was late — thousands of digital marketing agencies already in Australia. Cost per click for "SEO agency" / "Google Ads agency" was ~$36 a click; I couldn't compete with firms of 60–70 people spending $150k/month. I saved $7,000 and called a radio station wanting morning and afternoon drive-time ads. Their rate card was ~$500 a spot — I couldn't afford it. So I wired the sales guy $7,000: "I can't pay $500 a spot; I'll pay $150. Whenever you can't sell your ad inventory, run my spot. If in a month you haven't, send me back my $7,000." I recorded the creative on a ghetto Blue Yeti mic on my desk — a blind read. Those spots ran, and boy did they run. 700 leads in the first month. I had an outrageous business name — King Kong — while everyone else was "best web results" and generic digital-marketing names. Because I advertised in a market where none of my competitors were, my cut-through was sky-high. Sometimes the best move is not to copy the biggest competitors but to do the absolute opposite.

**Brutal truth #9 — Make it so simple even a chimpanzee could deliver on it.** Operate, streamline, and systemize your business with SOPs and checklists so simple a chimpanzee could run it. Think McDonald's — a 16-year-old can be a branch manager because it's systematized so well it runs without the best staff. I did every position in my business, then created a checklist for each, knowing that if all these things were done the results would be twice as good as any competitor. What are the 20% of inputs that deliver 80% of the result? How do I make it impossible to screw those up? If they fumbled everything else but got those 20% right, we'd still get 80% of the way there. First, master your craft — become better than anyone in your marketplace at that one craft. I sat in my bedroom obsessing: how do I write the best offers, the best copy, design the cleanest funnels? I did it laboriously, better than anyone, then systematized those 20% and hired people. That's how we got clients better results than any competitor — by focusing on the things that move the money needle.

**Brutal truth #10 — No one's opinion ever pays your bills.** When you start to see success, you get attention, and people start talking about you. If you get concerned with others' opinions you'll never want to leave your house. If you don't have critics or haters, the party ain't popping. No one's opinion of me ever paid my bills, so I don't care what my competitors say. The only thing I care about is: am I helping my customers, am I changing their lives, am I delivering value? A one-star review will kick you in the guts, but it's physically impossible to please everyone. Exercise: I ask a founder their favorite restaurant, then show them a one-star Google review of it — "you can't please everybody, right?" Even the finest kitchens in the world have food returned. For every person who loves a place, someone else will trash it. Instead, brainwash yourself with the lives you've changed — read your five-star reviews, look at your case studies and video testimonials. My office is like a shrine for client results. Whatever business you're in, I assume it's hard — if it weren't, everyone would be doing it and everyone would be millionaires. So forget everyone's opinion except your customers'. Don't let one or two negative stories cloud the judgment of the lives you're transforming.

**Brutal truth #11 — The hard road is the right road.** As an owner you make hundreds, if not thousands, of decisions a month. A mental model that has helped me: every decision splits like a fork in the road — the easy path and the hard path. I could spend more on ads now (less money in the bank — a negative second-order consequence), but that gets more leads and more money in the bank maybe three months from now. Look at every decision and ask what's the hard road, because the hard road gets you where you want to go. It's very difficult to build a real business, fit out an office, invest in a team, foster a culture — you can't just copy and paste these things, which is exactly why they become a moat and let you win long-term instead of short-term. Whenever you're faced with a decision, ask which is the hard road — that's the road to walk — because it's the opposite of what most people do, since people don't like doing hard things. Chances are you already know what you need to do in your business.

These are the brutal truths I've paid for in full. I have the battle scars to prove it.
